original_abstract: This article examines the historical and theological significance of the partnership between the African Independent Churches Association (AICA) and the Umphumulo Lutheran Theological College (ULTC) in South Africa. Central to this study is the African Independent Churches' (AICs) commitment to formalizing leadership training as a means of preserving doctrinal integrity while integrating indigenous spiritual experiences with systematic theological education. The research traces the collaborative efforts that led the AICA to seek training within mainline denominational frameworks, such as the ULTC, following the logistical and financial complexities involved in managing the AICA Theological College. By analyzing the evolution of this partnership, the article highlights the successes and systemic challenges faced by AIC students, including curriculum relevance and the broader role of the Lutheran World Federation in supporting indigenous church growth. Ultimately, the paper underscores the critical importance of ecumenical cooperation in developing a pastoral leadership capable of addressing the unique socio-religious needs of South African communities.
humanized_abstract: Abstract The relationship between the African Independent Churches Association (AICA) and the Umphumulo Lutheran Theological College (ULTC) in South Africa is analysed within the historical and theological framework. Focus is made on the fact that the AICs strive for leadership training in order to protect the doctrine whilst integrating indigenous religious experience with academic theological studies. The study describes the process of how the AICA collaborated with mainline denomination through going for studies at the ULTC after realising the complications in managing the AICA Theological College. The study reflects on the AICA students’ experiences as they underwent studies at the ULTC and on the challenges they encountered, as well as on the challenges related to the Lutheran World Federation (LWF)’s role towards the development of the AICs. Finally, the study strongly argues for ecumenical cooperation with a view to train a competent pastoral leadership in order to meet the socio-religious needs of the South African society.
